epigraph.quote: You cannot play a character you do not know. You can imitate one. You can indicate one. But you cannot play one. Playing requires knowing. And knowing requires excavation.

blocks.text: INT. LIBRARY — NIGHT
A blank page on the table. And a list of questions Brother Jack has written. Long.
BROTHER JACK
Before you speak the first line — you must know who is speaking it.
a beat
Not the facts. The biography in the script.
The wound. The thing that happened before the play began that made this person who they are when the play finds them.
a long beat
Every human being has a wound. An experience — chosen or unchosen — that shaped the architecture of how they move through the world.
Your character has one. Even if the writer never named it.
Especially if the writer never named it.
a beat
Your job is to find it.
INT. LIBRARY — CONTINUOUS
BROTHER JACK
The wound is what happened. The scar is what the person built over it.
a beat
Most characters are defined not by their wound but by their scar. The defence. The strategy. The personality they developed to survive what happened.
a beat
And underneath the scar — the want.
The deepest want. The one they will never say directly.
The one that drives every choice they make in every scene.
a long beat
She wants to be chosen. Not kept. Not tolerated. Not managed.
Chosen.
a beat
That want — underneath everything she says — is the character.
The lines are how she tries to get it.
INT. LIBRARY — LATER
BROTHER JACK
One more thing. The biggest secret.
a beat
The thing your character will never tell anyone. In the play or in their life.
Not a plot secret. A personal one.
The thing they would die before admitting.
a beat
Write it. On a piece of paper. Fold it. Keep it.
Tell no one.
a long beat
That secret is the engine of every scene.
The audience will never know it explicitly.
the close
But they will feel it.
In every pause. In every look. In every line that costs the character something.
The character you build in this lesson — the wound, the scar, the want, the secret — carries through every Phase Three lesson that follows. Do not rush this excavation.
The secret especially. Write it down. Keep it. Carry it into every scene. It will change the quality of every silence you hold.

requirement.prompt: Post your character's wound. Not the backstory — the wound underneath it. The thing that happened that shaped everything else. One paragraph. Honest. Include #JoelhoodP3L2.
